pattern: Once you fix which stone gets removed at each turn, the remaining sub-row is a smaller version of the same game, so the optimal score gap over any interval only depends on the two choices of which end to drop next.
hints: At any point only a contiguous sub-row of stones remains, so think of the problem as a function of that sub-row's two endpoints rather than the whole array.

For the sub-row from index i to j, whoever moves first can drop the left stone (scoring the sum of i+1..j, then facing the opponent's optimal gap on that smaller sub-row) or drop the right stone (scoring the sum of i..j-1, then facing the opponent's optimal gap on that sub-row) -- they pick whichever leaves them further ahead.
Precompute prefix sums so any sub-row sum is O(1), fill the dp over sub-row length from smallest to largest, and the final answer is the gap for the entire array from index 0 to n-1.

edge_inputs.description: all stones share the same value
one dominant stone in the middle tempts an early removal that is not optimal
strictly increasing values reward removing from the low end first
every stone at the maximum value

stress.description: random stone values near the length bound
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 500))
    stones = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [stones]
stress.note: kept well under the 1000-length constraint so the O(n^2) oracle finishes quickly
